region_of_origin: South Asia / Southeast Asia (widely cultivated globally)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of ground ginger is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.3 g), used as a spice in cooking, baking, or beverages.

nutrient_source.title: Ground ginger — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for ground ginger (dried root, powdered). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02021 (Spices, ginger, ground) for validation. HPB values used as primary source per regional source priority policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of spices and condiments — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on dried spice powders
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ground ginger does not have a directly published GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 10 (low) based on: (1) the food is used in very small culinary quantities (typical serving ~2.3 g); (2) net available carbohydrate per serving is approximately 1.3 g after fibre subtraction; (3) dried spice powders with high fibre content and bioactive compounds (gingerols, shogaols) are consistently classified as low-GI foods in peer-reviewed literature; (4) analogous dried spice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., cinnamon, turmeric) are all classified low-GI. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (10 × 1.3) / 100 ≈ 0.13, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (27 mg/100 g), exceptionally high potassium (1320 mg/100 g), and vasodilatory gingerols
hypertension.guidance: Ground ginger is highly suitable for hypertension management: its sodium content is negligible at culinary serving sizes (~0.6 mg per teaspoon), its potassium-to-sodium ratio is strongly favourable for blood pressure regulation, and gingerols have demonstrated vasodilatory and ACE-inhibitory properties in clinical studies; patients should avoid ginger-flavoured commercial products that may contain added salt or sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Hypoglycaemic gingerols, high dietary fibre (14.1 g/100 g), negligible glycaemic load per culinary serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ground ginger is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: multiple randomised controlled trials demonstrate that gingerols improve insulin sensitivity and reduce fasting blood glucose; at a typical culinary serving of ~2.3 g, the glycaemic load is approximately 0.2, making the carbohydrate contribution clinically negligible; patients should avoid ginger-flavoured products with added sugars such as ginger candy or sweetened ginger beverages.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory gingerols and shogaols, zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (1.24 g/100 g), high potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground ginger is cardioprotective at culinary doses: gingerols and shogaols inhibit platelet aggregation and reduce LDL oxidation, cholesterol is zero, saturated fat is low, and potassium at 1320 mg/100 g supports vascular health; patients on anticoagulant therapy (e.g., warfarin) should limit high-dose ginger supplementation and consult their clinician, though standard culinary quantities are safe.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: COX-2 and 5-LOX inhibition by gingerols and shogaols, zero trans fat, high potassium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground ginger is one of the most evidence-supported anti-inflammatory spices for rheumatoid arthritis: gingerols and shogaols directly inhibit COX-2 and 5-LOX enzymes, reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene production that drives joint inflammation; culinary quantities are safe, but high-dose ginger supplements may interact with anticoagulants used in RA management and should be discussed with a clinician.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground ginger is a finely milled, tan-coloured powder produced by drying and grinding the rhizome of Zingiber officinale. It has a warm, pungent, slightly sweet aroma and a sharp, spicy flavour that is more concentrated and less fresh-tasting than raw ginger root. The drying and grinding process concentrates the bioactive compounds — primarily gingerols (which partially convert to shogaols during drying) — that are responsible for both its distinctive flavour and its well-documented pharmacological properties. Ground ginger is a staple spice across South Asian, Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, and Western baking traditions, used in curries, spice blends (such as garam masala and five-spice), gingerbread, biscuits, marinades, and herbal teas. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 335 kcal, 8.98 g protein, 4.24 g fat, 71.62 g carbohydrate, 14.1 g dietary fibre, 27 mg sodium, and 1320 mg potassium. However, typical culinary servings are approximately 1–2 teaspoons (2–5 g), making the per-100 g macronutrient figures non-representative of actual dietary intake. The glycaemic load per teaspoon serving is approximately 0.2 — clinically negligible. Clinically, ground ginger has strong evidence for hypoglycaemic effects (improved insulin sensitivity, reduced fasting blood glucose), anti-inflammatory effects relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease (COX-2 and 5-LOX inhibition, platelet aggregation inhibition), and vasodilatory effects relevant to hypertension management. It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. It is widely used in Singapore, Malaysian, Indian, and global cuisines as a foundational spice.

meal_context: spice rub for meat and poultry

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved regional source) with confidence 0.95, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02021 (Spices, ginger, ground). The food identity is unambiguous. GI is an analogous estimate documented with full reasoning. Ontology codes are defensible and cross-checked.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.24 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.88 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated fat (1.07 g/100 g) values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02021 (Spices, ginger, ground) as the HPB database does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; this is documented as an adapted source for these sub-fields. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02021. 3. Vitamin A (mcg RAE) set to -1: not reported in HPB database; USDA reports only as IU (beta-carotene equivalent) without RAE conversion available for this entry. 4. Vitamin E set to -1: not reported in HPB database; limited data in USDA for this specific form. 5. Selenium set to -1: not reported in HPB database or USDA SR Legacy for ground ginger. 6. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA; ground ginger is a plant-derived spice and inherently contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). 7. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 and Vitamin D is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived spice with no animal-derived components. 8. GI value of 10 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for ground ginger exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 9. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' from the input record has been removed as ground ginger is a spice, not a grain product. 10. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1 or 0 where noted above; all -1 fields reflect data not available in primary HPB source and not reliably reported in USDA SR Legacy for this entry.
